title: Quantum Thermal Logic Gates
authors: Shuvadip Ghosh, Arnab Ghosh, Bivas Dutta, Papiya Maity
abstract: We propose a new concept for quantum thermal logic gates -- analogous to classical electronic logic gates -- that exploit the heat current in a coupled quantum-dot system tunnel-coupled to metallic thermal reservoirs for logic operations in quantum circuits. We obtained a remarkable one-to-one correspondence with the structure of classical electronic logic gate circuits. An experimental setup is presented that demonstrates a realizable nano-electronic quantum circuit architecture for implementing such quantum thermal logic operations.
summary: The authors propose and theoretically analyze Quantum Thermal Logic Gates (QTLGs) implemented using coupled quantum dots connected to thermal reservoirs. By calculating steady-state heat currents, they demonstrate that the system's response exhibits a perfect mapping to classical logic functions like AND, OR, and NOT. This work suggests a pathway for ultra-low power computation by utilizing controlled heat flow in nanoscale electronic circuits.
main_problem: The paper proposes developing quantum thermal logic gates (QTLGs) that mimic classical electronic logic operations using heat current in nanoscale quantum systems.
main_result: A one-to-one correspondence is established between QTLG operations and classical logic gates (AND, OR, NOT, NAND), demonstrating the feasibility of energy-efficient computation via thermal effects.
method: The analysis uses the Lindblad master equation to model time evolution, deriving steady-state heat and particle currents from transition rates governed by temperature gradients.
model_or_system: The physical system is a coupled quantum-dot (CQD) structure tunnel-coupled to multiple metallic thermal reservoirs (leads). The dynamics are described by the Lindblad master equation acting on the reduced density matrix of the dots.
key_observables: Heat current ($J^Q$) and particle current ($J^N$), which are compared against predefined thresholds ($J_0^Q, J_1^Q$) to determine the logic output (logic-0 or logic-1).
important_parameters: Input logic states are defined by reservoir temperatures ($T_S \lesssim 50 	ext{ mK}$ for logic-0; $T_S \gtrsim 200 	ext{ mK}$ for logic-1); the system relies on temperature gradients to drive heat flow.
main_assumptions: The model assumes weak coupling (Born-Markov approximation) and that inter-dot particle hopping is prohibited in certain analyses. The logic operation depends critically on the directionality of heat flow (thermal diode effect).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematics of the QTLG setups, showing operational cycles for Buffer, NOT, OR, AND, and NAND gates, with truth tables derived from measured heat current dependence on input temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the theoretical framework by deriving steady-state currents using rate equations. It then applies this formalism to construct and analyze fundamental gates (Buffer, NOT) before extending the concept to complex double-input gates (OR, AND, NOR, NAND).
why_interesting: This work bridges quantum thermodynamics with computation, showing how energy dissipation (heat flow) can be harnessed as a controllable information carrier in solid-state quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Schematic of the proposed QTLG: the CQD system is tunnel-coupled to S1 (hot/cold, here hot), S2 (hot/cold, here cold), and D (cold) leads; source tem- peratures define input thermal-logic (hot (red): 1, cold (blue): 0). I and C leads, coupled to QDa and QDb, are kept at high temperature for NOT and AND gate operations, respectively. The thermal current measured at D (or at I lead for NOT gates) defines the output thermal-logic. (b) The basic building block of a QTLG – a thermal-diode in the forward-bias configuration, where input logic-1 (broadened FD-distribution) allows heat- flow from S to D. Inset: CQD energy levels and its states.

FIG. 2: (a) CQD setup for the QTBG, with its equiva- lent electrical-diode circuit (Inset). (b) |JD Q| w.r.t input- temperature TS, for forward-bias µD = +21 µeV and µS = −21 µeV. (c) QTBG operational cycle for 1 →1 and (d) 0 →0; The net transition-cycle runs in ⟳di- rection; while the anti-clockwise cycle (⟲) is suppressed, as no hot-lead is coupled to QDb [See Appendix- C 2 for details].

FIG. 3: (a) QTNG setup and its electrical circuit (Inset). (b) JI Q w.r.t TS for the same parameters as buffer gate, with additional parameter µI = −21 µeV, kept below the QDa level, maintaining forward-bias. (c) QTNG opera- tional cycle for 0 →1 and (d) 1 →0. Like in QTBG, the net transition-cycle runs in ⟳direction; while the anti-clockwise cycle is suppressed (Appendix C 2). Here, the widths of the arrows indicate the relative strength of the heat-flow.

FIG. 4: Double-input QTLG setup and the correspond- ing 2D thermal-current plot w.r.t. the input T-logic for all four gates. Common parameters: γ = 3 GHz, εa = 10 µeV, εb = 15 µeV, U = 12 µeV, TD = 20 mK. Addi- tional parameters for (a) OR gate µS1 = µS2 = −25 µeV, µD = 21 µeV; (b) AND gate: µS1 = µS2 = 16 µeV, µC = −72 µeV, µD = 14 µeV; (c) NOR gate: µS1 = µS2 = −95 µeV, µI = −90 µeV, µD = 21 µeV; and (d) NAND gate: µS1 = µS2 = 16 µeV, µC = −16 µeV, µI = 16 µeV, µD = 9 µeV. The equivalent electrical cir- cuit for each gate is shown in the top inset, and output logic states are indicated on the color plots.
FIG. 5: Schematic of the proposed device, with two QD- junctions, integrated with a local heater and thermome- ters (cyan). The QD levels can be tuned by the corre- sponding back-gates.
